cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out the answer to this question. The question is asking when an ordinary tropical storm is called a hurricane. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I'll look back at the passage provided. In the first paragraph, it says: "many powerful storms are born in the tropical Atlantic and Caribbean seas. Of these, only about half a dozen becomes the strong, circling winds of 75 miles per hour or more that are called hurricanes..." So right here, it mentions that when the winds reach 75 miles per hour or more, they're called hurricanes. Let me check the options again. Option D says "When its winds reach 75 miles per hour." That seems to match exactly what the passage states. Looking at the other options to be sure. Option A says when it begins in the Atlantic and Caribbean seas. But the passage says that many storms start there, but only some become hurricanes. So the location isn't the determining factor, but the wind speed is. Option B is when it hits the coastline. The passage mentions that several hurricanes make their way to the coast and cause damage, but the name hurricane isn't given
